test_text.title: Getting from Toronto Airport to Milton

audioscript.text: Section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a telephone conversation between a passenger and an official at the Land Transport Information Service at Toronto Airport.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, this is Land Transport Information at Toronto Airport.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: How may I help you?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, good morning.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Um. I'm flying to Toronto airport next week, and I need to get to a town called Milton, could you tell me how I can get there?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Milton, did you say?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Let me see, I think that's about 150 miles southwest of here.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: In fact, it's 147 miles to be exact.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: So it'll take you at least say three to four hours by road.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: The distance from the airport to Milton is 147 miles, so 147 has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: And answer questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, this is Land Transport Information at Toronto Airport.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: How may I help you?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, good morning.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Um. I'm flying to Toronto airport next week, and I need to get to a town called Milton, could you tell me how I can get there?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Milton, did you say?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Let me see, I think that's about 150 miles southwest of here.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: In fact, it's 147 miles to be exact.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: So it'll take you at least say three to four hours by road.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Wow!
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Is it as far as that?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, I'm afraid so, but you have a number of options to get you there, and you can always rent a car right here at the airport of course.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Right, well, I don't really want to drive myself, so I'd like more information about public transport.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: OK, in that case the quickest and most comfortable is a cab, and of course there are always plenty available, but it'll cost you.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: You can also take a Greyhound bus or there's an airport shuttle service to Milton.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Hmm. I think for that kind of distance, a cab would be way beyond my budget.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: But the bus sounds OK.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Can you tell me how much that would cost?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Sure.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Let's see, that would be $15 one way.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Or $27.50 return.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Uh. That's on the Greyhound.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Ah, that's quite cheap, great.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Uh. Whereabouts does it stop in Milton?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It goes directly from the airport here to the city center, and it's pretty fast.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: But you have to bear in mind that there is only one departure a day, so it depends what time your flight gets in.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, of course.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Hang on,
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: we're due to get there at 11:30 am.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Hmm. Too bad, the bus leaves at 3:45, so you would have quite a wait, more than 4 hours.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, I see.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, what about the shuttle you mentioned?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: OK, that's the airport shuttle that will take you from the airport right to your hotel or private address.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It's a door to door service, and it would suit you much better, because there's one every two hours.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: So how much does that cost?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Let's see yeah, that's $35 one way, $65 return.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: So I guess it's a bit more expensive than the Greyhound.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, that doesn't sound too bad.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Especially if it'll take me straight to the hotel.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: But you do need to reserve a seat.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: OK, is it possible to make a booking right now through you?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Hmm, sure.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: OK, I just have to fill this form out for you.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: So, what date do you want to book this for?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: The 16th of October.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh no.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Sorry, that's my departure date.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I arrive on the 17th, so book it for then, please.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: So that's the Toronto Airport Shuttle to Milton. And this is for just one person or...
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, just me, please.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And you said your expected time of arrival was 11:30, so if I book your shuttle for after 12, let's say 12:30, that should give you plenty of time to you know collect your baggage, maybe grab a coffee.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yeah, that sounds fine, as long as we land on time.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, we'll take your flight details, so you don't need to worry too much about that.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: What about the fare?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: What sort of ticket do you want?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: One way or...
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, that'll be fine, provided I can book the return trip once I'm there.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: No problem.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Just allow a couple of days in advance to make sure you get a seat.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And what is your name please?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Janet, Janet Thomson.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Is that Thompson spelled with a 'p'?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: No, it's THOMSON.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: OK, and you'll be coming from the UK.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Uh. What flight will you be traveling on?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, uh, it's Air Canada flight number AC936 from London Heathrow.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Now, do you know where you'll be staying?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: We need to give the driver an address.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, it's called the Vacation Motel, and I think it's near the town center.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Anyway, the address is 24, Kitchener Street, that's KITCHENER Street.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That's fine.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Right, so that's $35 to pay please.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Have you got your credit card number there?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, it's a visa card, and the number is 3303845020456837.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: OK, well that seems to be everything.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Have a good trip, and we'll see you in Toronto next week.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, bye.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, thanks for your help.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items: Address in Milton: Vacation Motel, 24, Kitchener Street
body.title: AIRPORT SHUTTLE BOOKING FORM

answer_key.correct_answer: taxi | cab | a taxi | a cab

test_text.title: PS Camping: Company Overview and Holiday Offerings

audioscript.text: Section 2, you will hear a representative from a holiday company called PS Camping, giving a talk about the holidays the company organizes.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Thank you all for coming to my talk this evening.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's nice to see so many people in the audience.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For those of you who don't know very much about PS Camping, let me start by giving you some background information about the company.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The company started 25 years ago.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It actually opened as a retail chain selling camping equipment, and then 20 years ago, it bought a small number of camp sites in the UK.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And began offering camping holidays.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The company grew rapidly, and has been providing holidays in continental Europe for the last 15 years.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you book a camping holiday with us, you'll have a choice of over 300 sites.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In Italy, we now have some 64 sites that we either own or have exclusive use of. France is where we have the majority of sites, and we currently have a project to expand into Switzerland.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We also have a number of sites in northern Spain, particularly in the mountainous region of Picos de Europa.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We've upgraded all these Spanish sites, and improved them considerably from their original 3 star rating.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We believe our holidays offer superb facilities for the whole family.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Parents who want their children to be fully occupied for all or part of the day can take advantage of our children's activities.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These are organized by our well qualified and enthusiastic staff.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Each day kicks off with a sports match.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Perhaps football or volleyball, followed by an hour of drama for everyone.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This may include singing or dancing, MIME or other activities.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the afternoon, there's a different art activity for each day of the week, including a poster competition or model making.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: What's more, our sites are truly child friendly, and with this in mind, we operate a no noise rule in the evenings.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Children's evening activities usually finish at 9:30 or occasionally 10, and from 10:30 holiday makers are expected to be quiet.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the areas where there are tents.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We want nothing to go wrong on a PS Camping holiday, but if it does, we also want all our customers to be insured.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you haven't organized an annual insurance policy of your own,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: you'll need to take out the low cost cover we offer, and we require that you arrange this when you make your holiday reservation.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are many advantages to choosing PS Camping.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And to recommending it to others, as a regular customer, you'll be kept informed of special offers, and your friends can benefit from 10% off their holiday.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Or book a luxury tent for the price of a standard one.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In return, we'll send you a thank you present, which you can choose from a list of high quality items.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: When it comes to our tents, these are equipped to the highest standard.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We really do think of every essential detail, from an oven and cooking rings fueled by bottled gas.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To mirrors in the bedroom areas.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you don't want to cook indoors, you can borrow a barbecue.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you ask in advance for one to be made available, and there's even a picnic blanket to sit on outside your tent.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Inside, a box of games and toys can be found, and children's tents can be hired if required.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: All tents have a fridge, and if you want to spend the day on the beach for example, ask for a specially designed PS Camping cool box, which will keep your food and drinks chilled.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are excellent washing facilities at all our sites.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: With washing machines and clothes lines in the central areas, along with mops and buckets, in case your tent needs cleaning during your stay.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: All sites have a cafe and/or a shop.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For those who'd rather eat in than dine at a local restaurant.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 2, you will hear a representative from a holiday company called PS Camping, giving a talk about the holidays the company organizes.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 16.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 11 to 16.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Thank you all for coming to my talk this evening.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's nice to see so many people in the audience.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For those of you who don't know very much about PS Camping, let me start by giving you some background information about the company.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The company started 25 years ago.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It actually opened as a retail chain selling camping equipment, and then 20 years ago, it bought a small number of camp sites in the UK.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And began offering camping holidays.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The company grew rapidly, and has been providing holidays in continental Europe for the last 15 years.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: If you book a camping holiday with us, you'll have a choice of over 300 sites.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In Italy, we now have some 64 sites that we either own or have exclusive use of. France is where we have the majority of sites, and we currently have a project to expand into Switzerland.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We also have a number of sites in northern Spain, particularly in the mountainous region of Picos de Europa.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We've upgraded all these Spanish sites, and improved them considerably from their original 3 star rating.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We believe our holidays offer superb facilities for the whole family.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Parents who want their children to be fully occupied for all or part of the day can take advantage of our children's activities.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These are organized by our well qualified and enthusiastic staff.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Each day kicks off with a sports match.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Perhaps football or volleyball, followed by an hour of drama for everyone.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This may include singing or dancing, MIME or other activities.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In the afternoon, there's a different art activity for each day of the week, including a poster competition or model making.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: What's more, our sites are truly child friendly, and with this in mind, we operate a no noise rule in the evenings.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Children's evening activities usually finish at 9:30 or occasionally 10, and from 10:30 holiday makers are expected to be quiet.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In the areas where there are tents.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We want nothing to go wrong on a PS Camping holiday, but if it does, we also want all our customers to be insured.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: If you haven't organized an annual insurance policy of your own,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: you'll need to take out the low cost cover we offer, and we require that you arrange this when you make your holiday reservation.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There are many advantages to choosing PS Camping.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And to recommending it to others, as a regular customer, you'll be kept informed of special offers, and your friends can benefit from 10% off their holiday.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Or book a luxury tent for the price of a standard one.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In return, we'll send you a thank you present, which you can choose from a list of high quality items.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: When it comes to our tents, these are equipped to the highest standard.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We really do think of every essential detail, from an oven and cooking rings fueled by bottled gas.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: To mirrors in the bedroom areas.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: If you don't want to cook indoors, you can borrow a barbecue.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: If you ask in advance for one to be made available, and there's even a picnic blanket to sit on outside your tent.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Inside, a box of games and toys can be found, and children's tents can be hired if required.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: All tents have a fridge, and if you want to spend the day on the beach for example, ask for a specially designed PS Camping cool box, which will keep your food and drinks chilled.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There are excellent washing facilities at all our sites.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: With washing machines and clothes lines in the central areas, along with mops and buckets, in case your tent needs cleaning during your stay.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: All sites have a cafe and/or a shop.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For those who'd rather eat in than dine at a local restaurant.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: PS Camping has been organising holidays for
The company has most camping sites in
Which organised activity can children do every day of the week?
Some areas of the sites have a 'no noise' rule after
The holiday insurance that is offered by PS Camping
options: can be charged on an annual basis.
is included in the price of the holiday.
must be taken out at the time of booking.

options: an upgrade to a luxury tent.

text: What does the speaker say about the following items?
textReadable: What does the speaker say about the following items?

list: They are provided in all tents.
They are found in central areas of the campsite.

test_text.title: Managing Individual Differences in the Workplace

audioscript.text: Section 3, you will hear a tutor talking to two business students called Philip and Janice about their research on managing individuals.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 23.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 23.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Well, you've both been looking at different styles of managing individuals in companies and the workplace.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: How's the research going, Philip?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Well I've been looking at why individualism, I mean individual differences are such an important area of management studies.
audioscript.speaker: PHILIP

audioscript.text: When you think about any organization, be it a family business or a multinational company.
audioscript.speaker: PHILIP

audioscript.text: They're all fundamentally a group of people working together, but it's what these individuals contribute to their places of work that makes you realize how important they are.
audioscript.speaker: PHILIP

audioscript.text: Of course, they bring different ideas.
audioscript.speaker: PHILIP

audioscript.text: But it's also their attitudes and their experiences of learning.
audioscript.speaker: PHILIP

audioscript.text: Diversity is important in these areas too.
audioscript.speaker: PHILIP

audioscript.text: So, why do people behave so differently from one another at work?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: There are lots of reasons, but research has shown that a lot of it comes down to personality, and the other factor is gender.
audioscript.speaker: PHILIP

audioscript.text: It's a well-known fact that men and women do lots of things in different ways.
audioscript.speaker: PHILIP

audioscript.text: And the workplace is no different.
audioscript.speaker: PHILIP

audioscript.text: Did you look at the effects of this variation on companies?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yes, I did.
audioscript.speaker: PHILIP

audioscript.text: On the positive side, exposure to such diversity helps encourage creativity,
audioscript.speaker: PHILIP

audioscript.text: which is generally an asset to a company.
audioscript.speaker: PHILIP

audioscript.text: But unfortunately, individual differences are also the root of conflict between staff, and they can lead to difficulties for management, which can sometimes be serious.
audioscript.speaker: PHILIP

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 24 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 24 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Thanks, Philip.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: So now I guess the two main things to remember here are to identify individual talent, and then to utilize it.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: So Janice, you were looking at identifying different talents in workers.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Do you think this is easy for managers to do?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Well currently teamwork is in fashion in the workplace.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: And in my opinion, the importance of the individual is generally neglected.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: What managers should be targeting is those employees who can take the lead in a situation, and are not afraid to accept the idea of responsibility.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Hmm, that's true, Janice, but unfortunately, many managers think the entire notion of encouraging individuality amongst their staff is far too hard.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yes, that may be true, but I think one of the most important tasks of managers is to consider the needs of the individual on one hand, and group cooperation and conformity on the other.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: It requires creative thinking on the part of management to avoid tension.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: So Janice, what kind of people do you think companies should be looking for?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Well, uh, it has to start from the very beginning, when companies are looking for new employees.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: When the personnel department is choosing between applicants, they need to look for someone who's broken the mold, and can think for themselves.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Instead, people making these decisions often use a range of psychological tests to see if a person is a problem solver, or will do as they're told.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: I'm not convinced these qualities are actually the most important.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: So do you think being a good team player is overrated?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: No, it's not overrated.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: You do need to learn the rules and learn them fast.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: No individual can get around this if they're working in an organization.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: So how should managers deal with this?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Rewards, when an individual demonstrates the behavior the organization expects, some kind of incentive can be given.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: What's important here is that this happens right at the beginning.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: So new recruits learn the rules of the system immediately.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Also the incentive should be something the individual actually wants, and this isn't always just money.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: To come back to you, Philip.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: You were saying that recognition of good performers is essential.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: What else should managers be looking for?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Well, managing people means you not only have an understanding of your employees.
audioscript.speaker: PHILIP

audioscript.text: But you also recognize the culture of the organization.
audioscript.speaker: PHILIP

audioscript.text: In fact, for some organizations creativity and individuality may be the last thing they want to see during working hours.
audioscript.speaker: PHILIP

audioscript.text: Very true.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yes, but managing people isn't as easy as it looks.
audioscript.speaker: PHILIP

audioscript.text: For example, change in the workplace can be quite tricky, especially if there's a need to increase profit.
audioscript.speaker: PHILIP

audioscript.text: And at times like these, managers may have to give priority to profit rather than individual staff needs.
audioscript.speaker: PHILIP

audioscript.text: Hmm. Yes, and that creates difficult situations for people.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: PHILIP

audioscript.text: But what's important is that managers are able to deal with quite high levels of personal stress. During times of change, they should be thinking not only about the strain on their staff, but take time out to think of themselves.
audioscript.speaker: PHILIP

audioscript.text: Absolutely.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: So what are the implications of that for...
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

test_text.title: Preparing for the Archaeological Fieldwork in Namibia

audioscript.text: Section 4, you will hear part of a lecture about cave paintings and other types of rock art.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good afternoon everyone.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is the first seminar in preparation for our archaeological fieldwork in Namibia.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We are fantastically lucky to have received partial research funding for this trip from our institute.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So I shall expect 200% attention and participation from you all.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: First in this seminar, I'm going to give a brief introduction to contemporary research on rock art, and in the second part, I'm going to give you some do's and don'ts for our field work trip in April.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So please listen very carefully.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I'm first going to focus on the interpretation of rock art in Namibia.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We are very fortunate to be going to an area where you can find some of the most important sites in the entire world.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And I hope to show you how easy it is for everyone to make mistakes in looking at cultures which are different from our own, the first and most important lesson we have to learn.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In Namibia, there are both paintings and engravings.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That's where the surface of the rock is cut out.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Many of the engravings show footprints of animals, and most scholars used to think that the purpose of these was simple and obvious.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This rock art was like a school book with pictures to teach children about tracks, which track belonged to which animal, giraffe, lion, and so on.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But there were some mysteries.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: First, when you look at a typical Namibian painting or engraving, you see the tracks are repeated.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are dozens of tracks for the same animal.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, you'd expect just one clear illustration if the reason - the aim - was to teach tracking.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now, there were two more problems.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Why are some of the engravings of animals very accurate as you'd expect?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: All clearly identifiable, and others quite unrealistic.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And another mystery, some of these unrealistic animals that's in the engravings seem to be half human.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Some for example have got human faces.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Many researchers now think that these were pictures the wise men engraved of themselves.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They believe they could use magic to control the animals they had drawn, so the hunters could then catch them for food.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This shows you some of the dangers of coming from one culture to another, as we'll be doing without understanding it fully.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Scholars imagined that children looked at rock art pictures to learn to track.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Just because they themselves had learned skills from pictures.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Many researchers now believe that rock art had a much more complex purpose, and we'll talk more about it next week.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now, before I invite you to join in a discussion in this second part of the seminar, I'd like to make some very important points about our field work, and in fact any field trip to look at rock art.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We're going to a number of sites, and we won't always be together.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The single largest problem faced by people who manage the sites is, yes, I'm sure you've guessed, damage caused by visitors.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Even though it's usually unintentional.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Whenever you do go to a site, don't forget you can learn many things from observing at a distance, instead of walking all over it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This can really help to reduce visitor pressure.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: People often say, well there's only two of us, and just this one time, but maybe thousands of people are saying the same thing.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And then some basic rules to guide you.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We'll have our own camp near a village, but remember never to camp on a site if you go on your own.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It may be disrespectful to the people of that culture.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And certainly don't make fires, however romantic it may seem.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's really dangerous in dry areas, and you can easily burn priceless undiscovered material by doing so.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, how are we going to enjoy the rock art on our field trip?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: By looking at it, drawing it, and photographing it, never by touching it or even tracing it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Rock art is fragile and precious.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Remember that climbing on rocks and in caves can destroy in a moment what has lasted for centuries.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, no heroics in Namibia please.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Try to be extra careful and help others to be too.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Oh, and lastly, please don't even move rocks or branches to take photographs.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You should leave the site intact.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I'm sure I can rely on you to do that.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, that's about all I want to say before today's first discussion, but if you have any questions, please ask them now.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Oh, and don't forget you'll find some fascinating information about worldwide sites on the Internet.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Right, first question then.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 4, you will hear part of a lecture about cave paintings and other types of rock art.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Good afternoon everyone.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This is the first seminar in preparation for our archaeological fieldwork in Namibia.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We are fantastically lucky to have received partial research funding for this trip from our institute.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So I shall expect 200% attention and participation from you all.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: First in this seminar, I'm going to give a brief introduction to contemporary research on rock art, and in the second part, I'm going to give you some do's and don'ts for our field work trip in April.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So please listen very carefully.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I'm first going to focus on the interpretation of rock art in Namibia.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We are very fortunate to be going to an area where you can find some of the most important sites in the entire world.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And I hope to show you how easy it is for everyone to make mistakes in looking at cultures which are different from our own, the first and most important lesson we have to learn.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In Namibia, there are both paintings and engravings.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That's where the surface of the rock is cut out.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Many of the engravings show footprints of animals, and most scholars used to think that the purpose of these was simple and obvious.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This rock art was like a school book with pictures to teach children about tracks, which track belonged to which animal, giraffe, lion, and so on.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But there were some mysteries.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: First, when you look at a typical Namibian painting or engraving, you see the tracks are repeated.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There are dozens of tracks for the same animal.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Well, you'd expect just one clear illustration if the reason - the aim - was to teach tracking.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now, there were two more problems.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Why are some of the engravings of animals very accurate as you'd expect?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: All clearly identifiable, and others quite unrealistic.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And another mystery, some of these unrealistic animals that's in the engravings seem to be half human.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Some for example have got human faces.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Many researchers now think that these were pictures the wise men engraved of themselves.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They believe they could use magic to control the animals they had drawn, so the hunters could then catch them for food.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This shows you some of the dangers of coming from one culture to another, as we'll be doing without understanding it fully.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Scholars imagined that children looked at rock art pictures to learn to track.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Just because they themselves had learned skills from pictures.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Many researchers now believe that rock art had a much more complex purpose, and we'll talk more about it next week.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now, before I invite you to join in a discussion in this second part of the seminar, I'd like to make some very important points about our field work, and in fact any field trip to look at rock art.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We're going to a number of sites, and we won't always be together.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The single largest problem faced by people who manage the sites is, yes, I'm sure you've guessed, damage caused by visitors.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Even though it's usually unintentional.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Whenever you do go to a site, don't forget you can learn many things from observing at a distance, instead of walking all over it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This can really help to reduce visitor pressure.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: People often say, well there's only two of us, and just this one time, but maybe thousands of people are saying the same thing.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And then some basic rules to guide you.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We'll have our own camp near a village, but remember never to camp on a site if you go on your own.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It may be disrespectful to the people of that culture.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And certainly don't make fires, however romantic it may seem.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's really dangerous in dry areas, and you can easily burn priceless undiscovered material by doing so.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, how are we going to enjoy the rock art on our field trip?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: By looking at it, drawing it, and photographing it, never by touching it or even tracing it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Rock art is fragile and precious.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Remember that climbing on rocks and in caves can destroy in a moment what has lasted for centuries.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, no heroics in Namibia please.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Try to be extra careful and help others to be too.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Oh, and lastly, please don't even move rocks or branches to take photographs.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You should leave the site intact.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I'm sure I can rely on you to do that.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Well, that's about all I want to say before today's first discussion, but if you have any questions, please ask them now.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Oh, and don't forget you'll find some fascinating information about worldwide sites on the Internet.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Right, first question then.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER
